title: Inequality
summary: The unequal distribution of income, wealth, opportunity, and status among individuals and groups — a feature of every known society, whose causes, consequences, and moral significance are among the most contested questions in politics and economics.
hook: Every human society has had inequality. None has eliminated it. The question has never been whether inequality exists but how much, what kind, and whether any of it is just — and those questions have produced some of the most consequential political arguments in history.

beginner.theConcept:
Inequality means that some people have more than others — more money, more wealth, more opportunity, more power, more status. Every society that has ever existed has had inequality of some kind. The question is how much, and whether it is fair.

There are different kinds of inequality. Income inequality is the gap between how much different people earn each year. Wealth inequality is the gap in what people own — houses, savings, investments. These are different: a doctor might earn a high income but have little wealth if she is paying off medical school debt; an old family might have enormous wealth in land or stocks but relatively modest income.

There is also inequality of opportunity — whether a child born into a poor family has the same chance to succeed as a child born into a rich one. Most people in most countries believe that some inequality of outcome is acceptable but that inequality of opportunity is unfair. In practice, the two are connected: children of wealthy parents tend to have better schools, more connections, and more safety nets that make their own success more likely.

Inequality has been growing in many countries, particularly the United States, since the 1970s. The rich have gotten richer much faster than everyone else. The top 1% of Americans now own more wealth than the bottom 90% combined. Whether this is a problem — and if so, what to do about it — is one of the most active political debates of the present moment.
beginner.problemItAddresses:
Inequality addresses the basic question of how a society distributes what it produces. This question has never had a simple answer.

On one side, some inequality seems necessary for a productive economy. If everyone is paid the same regardless of effort, skill, or contribution, there is less reason to work hard, develop valuable skills, or take risks. Unequal rewards can create incentives that make the whole economy more productive — and a more productive economy might mean that even the poorest are better off than they would be in a more equal but less productive one.

On the other side, very high inequality seems to cause problems. Societies with more inequality tend to have more social problems: higher crime, lower trust, worse health outcomes, less social mobility. Very high inequality may also undermine democracy, because those with money can use it to influence politics in ways that serve their interests rather than the common good.

The question is not whether any inequality should exist — virtually everyone accepts some — but how much is too much, and what kinds of inequality are acceptable.
beginner.competingTraditions:
Two broad traditions have dominated thinking about inequality.

The first says that inequality is primarily a product of differences in individual talent, effort, and choice. People who work harder and develop more valuable skills earn more — and this is as it should be. On this view, the appropriate response to poverty is not redistribution but ensuring that everyone has the opportunity to succeed through their own efforts. Programs that transfer money from the wealthy to the poor may discourage work and create dependency.

The second tradition says that inequality is primarily a product of social structures — the circumstances people are born into, the power imbalances embedded in economic institutions, and the ways that wealth reproduces itself across generations. A child born into poverty faces obstacles that no amount of individual effort can fully overcome. On this view, redistribution through taxation and public services is not a distortion of natural outcomes but a correction of structural advantages that the wealthy did not earn.

Most people hold views that draw on both traditions — believing that individual effort matters but that opportunity is unequally distributed and that some redistribution is just.
beginner.politicalStakes:
Inequality is not just an economic fact. It has political consequences.

Wealthy people have more access to political power — they can donate to campaigns, hire lobbyists, and fund think tanks that promote their views. In a democracy, this creates a tension: political equality (one person, one vote) and economic inequality (some people have vastly more than others) can pull in opposite directions.

Research by political scientists suggests that in the United States, the policy preferences of wealthy Americans have much more influence on what government does than the preferences of ordinary citizens. If this is right, democracy is functioning less like 'one person, one vote' and more like a system in which money amplifies political voice.

There is also a relationship between economic inequality and political polarization. Research suggests that as inequality has grown, political conflict has intensified — that people in more economically precarious positions are more susceptible to political appeals based on resentment and fear. Whether inequality causes polarization, or both are products of the same underlying forces, is debated.
beginner.contestedHistoryC:
The history of inequality is itself contested — particularly the question of how much inequality there has been and what has driven its changes.

One contested claim is about the long run. Did inequality rise with agriculture and the emergence of surplus production, or did it emerge much earlier in hunter-gatherer societies? Evidence from skeletal remains, burial sites, and genetic studies suggests that substantial inequality — including marked differences in diet, health, and status — predates agriculture, though the scale and rigidity of hierarchy seem to have increased with it.

A second contested claim concerns the 20th century. Inequality fell dramatically in many rich countries between roughly 1914 and 1970 — driven by wars, depressions, progressive taxation, and the rise of unions. Whether this compression was a permanent structural change or a temporary disruption to long-run inequality patterns is debated. The rise in inequality since the 1970s suggests it may have been temporary.
beginner.whereDebateStands:
The empirical measurement of inequality has improved dramatically in recent decades, particularly through the work of economists Thomas Piketty, Emmanuel Saez, and Gabriel Zucman, who used tax records to document the concentration of income and wealth at the very top of the distribution in ways that surveys had missed.

The policy debate has several active fronts: whether to raise top marginal income tax rates, whether to impose wealth taxes on large fortunes, whether to expand public services that reduce inequality of opportunity (education, healthcare, childcare), and whether to strengthen labor market institutions (unions, minimum wages) that compress wage inequality.

The deepest disagreement is normative: how much inequality is too much, and why? Libertarians argue that inequality is just as long as it resulted from voluntary exchanges and that redistribution violates individual rights. Egalitarians argue that very high inequality is inherently unjust regardless of how it arose. Most political debate falls between these positions, accepting some redistribution while disagreeing about how much.

general.theConcept:
Inequality as an analytical concept has multiple dimensions that are often conflated in political debate. Income inequality — the dispersion of annual earnings — is the most frequently measured but least consequential dimension. Wealth inequality — the distribution of accumulated assets — is more fundamental because wealth confers security, opportunity, and political power that income alone does not. Consumption inequality — what people can actually afford to buy — is most directly relevant to living standards. Opportunity inequality — the extent to which birth circumstances determine life outcomes — is perhaps the most normatively significant.

These dimensions are related but not identical. A society could have relatively equal incomes while having very unequal wealth (if wealth differences persist from previous generations despite compressed current earnings). A society could have high income inequality but lower consumption inequality if the tax and transfer system redistributes aggressively. Understanding which dimension is being discussed is essential to any serious political argument about inequality.

The empirical foundation of the contemporary inequality debate was transformed by the work of Thomas Piketty, Emmanuel Saez, and their collaborators, who used administrative tax records rather than surveys to measure income and wealth concentration. Surveys systematically undercount top incomes because wealthy respondents underreport or because the very wealthy are too rare to appear in representative samples. Tax records capture this group directly. The result was a revision upward of measured inequality in the United States and other countries — particularly the discovery that the top 0.1% and 0.01% had captured an extraordinary share of income growth since the 1970s.
general.problemItAddresses:
Inequality addresses two distinct problems that are often run together: the descriptive problem of explaining why inequality varies across societies and over time, and the normative problem of determining how much inequality is just and what should be done about it.

The descriptive problem is primarily economic and sociological. Why did income inequality fall dramatically in the United States from roughly 1930 to 1970, then rise just as dramatically from the 1970s to the present? The leading explanations include skill-biased technological change (new technologies disproportionately rewarding workers with more education), globalization (competition from low-wage labor reducing wages for less-skilled workers in rich countries), the decline of unions (reducing workers' bargaining power), changes in corporate governance norms (increasing executive pay), and deliberate policy choices (tax cuts for high earners, reduced regulation of finance).

The normative problem is primarily philosophical. What makes inequality just or unjust? Luck egalitarians argue that inequalities due to choices people make are just, while inequalities due to circumstances beyond people's control are unjust. Rawlsians argue that inequalities are just only if they benefit the least well-off members of society. Libertarians argue that inequalities arising from voluntary exchanges are just regardless of outcomes. These frameworks generate radically different prescriptions from the same empirical facts.
general.competingTraditions:
The debate about inequality sits at the intersection of at least three independent intellectual traditions.

The liberal tradition, drawing on John Rawls's A Theory of Justice (1971), argues that a just society would be one chosen behind a 'veil of ignorance' — by people who did not know their place in it. Behind this veil, Rawls argued, rational people would choose a society that maximized the position of its least advantaged members. This generates a strong presumption in favor of redistribution and equality of opportunity, but not full equality of outcomes.

The libertarian tradition, drawing on Robert Nozick's Anarchy, State, and Utopia (1974), argues that any distribution of resources is just if it arose through voluntary exchanges from a just initial distribution. Redistribution through taxation violates the rights of those taxed. The appropriate response to inequality is ensuring that exchanges are voluntary and that the initial distribution was legitimate — not engineering particular outcomes.

The socialist and social democratic tradition argues that market distributions are not in fact voluntary in any meaningful sense because the alternatives available to workers and the poor are constrained by a property system they did not choose. Redistribution is not a correction of market outcomes but a partial reversal of the politically structured property rights that produce those outcomes.
general.politicalStakes:
The political stakes of inequality operate at multiple levels.

At the level of democratic governance, research by political scientists Larry Bartels and Martin Gilens suggests that US government policy is significantly more responsive to the preferences of wealthy Americans than to those of average citizens or the poor — a finding that challenges the basic premise of democratic equality. Whether this reflects the direct influence of money in politics, the cultural and social connections between wealthy donors and politicians, or simply that wealthy people's views are better informed is debated. But the empirical pattern is robust.

At the level of social cohesion, a large literature — most accessible in Richard Wilkinson and Kate Pickett's The Spirit Level (2009) — documents that societies with higher inequality tend to have worse outcomes on a wide range of social indicators: health, crime, trust, social mobility, educational achievement. The proposed mechanism is that high inequality increases status anxiety and social comparison, undermining the trust and cooperation that social institutions require. This literature is contested: critics argue that the correlations reflect confounding variables rather than inequality's independent effect.

At the level of macroeconomic stability, economists including Joseph Stiglitz and Raghuram Rajan have argued that high inequality contributed to the 2008 financial crisis — partly by reducing consumer demand in ways that were compensated by debt, and partly by concentrating financial wealth in ways that increased the scale and risk of financial speculation. This argument is influential but not universally accepted.
general.contestedHistoryC:
The long-run history of inequality is one of the most active areas of economic history. Piketty's Capital in the Twenty-First Century (2014) proposed a sweeping theory: that inequality is the natural state of capitalist economies (because the return on capital tends to exceed the growth rate of the economy), that the compression of inequality in the mid-20th century was a historically exceptional disruption caused by war and policy, and that without deliberate intervention, inequality will return to 19th-century levels or higher.

This argument has been criticized on empirical and theoretical grounds. Economists including Daron Acemoglu and David Autor have argued that Piketty's r > g formula is too simple to explain the actual pattern of inequality — which reflects specific mechanisms (labor market institutions, technological change, globalization) rather than a universal tendency of capitalism. The debate about the long-run drivers of inequality versus the specific institutional and policy determinants of its mid-century compression is still very much live.

A separate historical debate concerns the deep pre-history of inequality. Archaeological evidence from Walter Scheidel's The Great Leveler (2017) suggests that throughout most of recorded history, large-scale reductions in inequality have been produced almost exclusively by mass mobilization violence, pandemic, revolution, and state collapse — not by peaceful political reform. If Scheidel is right, the post-war welfare state is historically exceptional in having reduced inequality through political means rather than catastrophe.
general.whereDebateStands:
The empirical debate about inequality is more settled than the normative debate. There is strong consensus that income and wealth inequality have increased in most developed countries since the 1970s, that the increase has been most dramatic in the United States, and that it has been particularly concentrated at the very top of the distribution. There is less consensus about causes — the relative contribution of technology, globalization, institutions, and policy remains debated.

The policy debate is active across several fronts. Piketty's proposal for a global wealth tax has been influential as an agenda-setting idea but has not been adopted anywhere. More incremental proposals — higher top marginal income tax rates, inheritance tax reform, expanded public services, strengthened labor market institutions — are debated intensely in most rich democracies. The evidence on the effects of these policies is substantial but contested: there is genuine uncertainty about the magnitudes of behavioral responses to higher taxes, the long-run effects of different redistributive mechanisms, and the political feasibility of different reforms.

The deepest unresolved question is normative: how much inequality is too much? The empirical literature on the effects of inequality provides some answers — high inequality seems to have costs for health, trust, and social mobility that most people would consider bad. But the question of what inequality we would accept if there were no such costs — whether inequality of outcomes is intrinsically objectionable or only instrumentally so — is a philosophical question that economics cannot settle.

advanced.theConcept:
The contemporary inequality debate is structured by a tension between two frameworks that are rarely made explicit: a luck egalitarian framework that asks about the sources of inequality (distinguishing deserved from undeserved), and a sufficientarian or prioritarian framework that asks about the consequences of inequality (particularly for those at the bottom). These frameworks generate different questions and different policy prescriptions from the same empirical evidence.

Luck egalitarianism — associated with philosophers G.A. Cohen, Ronald Dworkin, and Eric Rakowski — holds that inequalities due to brute luck (circumstances beyond individual control) are unjust, while inequalities due to option luck (the results of informed choices) are not. This framework is intuitively appealing but generates paradoxes: it seems to require compensating people for bad choices if those choices were determined by circumstances beyond their control, leading to what Elizabeth Anderson called 'harsh treatment of the irresponsible poor.' The prioritarian alternative holds that what matters is not the source of inequality but the absolute position of the worst-off, with additional weight given to benefits to those at the bottom. This sidesteps the luck question but requires specifying the morally appropriate 'priority weight.'

Both frameworks avoid what may be the most important question: whether very high inequality is objectionable not because of its sources or its effects on the worst-off but because of what it does to the relationship between people — the way it undermines the equal standing and mutual respect that democratic citizenship requires. This relational egalitarianism, developed most fully by Anderson and Samuel Scheffler, focuses on social relationships rather than distributions and implies different policy conclusions than either luck egalitarianism or prioritarianism.
advanced.problemItAddresses:
The empirical problem of explaining the rise in inequality since the 1970s has generated a large and contentious literature. The major candidate explanations are not mutually exclusive, and their relative weights are still debated.

Skill-biased technological change (SBTC) was the dominant explanation in the 1990s: new technologies (particularly computers and software) disproportionately complemented high-skilled workers while substituting for routine tasks performed by middle-skilled workers. The evidence for SBTC is substantial — the return to a college education rose sharply during the period of rising inequality — but it cannot explain why inequality rose more in the United States than in other developed countries that adopted similar technologies.

The institutional explanation — developed by economists Lawrence Katz, Alan Krueger, and David Card — emphasizes the role of labor market institutions: union density declined from roughly 35% of the private sector workforce in the 1950s to under 7% today; the real minimum wage fell significantly relative to productivity; and executive pay norms changed dramatically, with CEO-to-worker pay ratios rising from roughly 30:1 in the 1960s to over 300:1 today. These institutional changes explain the US-specific patterns that SBTC cannot.

Piketty's capital-concentration argument adds a third mechanism: as the return to capital exceeds the growth rate of the economy, wealth accumulates faster than income, and since wealth is more concentrated than income, this increases both wealth and income inequality. Critics note that much of the observed increase in the capital share reflects housing values rather than productive capital, and that the r > g relationship has not been stable enough to bear the weight Piketty places on it.
advanced.competingTraditions:
The three major normative traditions — Rawlsian liberal egalitarianism, Nozickian libertarianism, and socialist political economy — have been joined by two more recent additions that are analytically important.

Capabilities approaches, associated with Amartya Sen and Martha Nussbaum, shift focus from the distribution of resources (income, wealth) to the distribution of what people can actually do and be — their capabilities. On this view, income inequality is instrumentally important because income enables capabilities, but the direct focus should be on whether people can live lives of dignity, participate in political life, maintain health, and develop their human potential. This framework generates different priorities than resource-focused approaches: capability inequality in health or education may be more urgent than income inequality even when income distributions are the same.

Relational egalitarianism, developed by Elizabeth Anderson, Samuel Scheffler, and Martin O'Neill, argues that the fundamental problem with inequality is not the distribution of resources per se but what high inequality does to social relationships. In a highly unequal society, the poor are not simply poor — they are humiliated, excluded, and denied the equal standing that democratic citizenship requires. This framework implies focusing on the social conditions of equality (access to public goods, elimination of demeaning dependencies) rather than on particular distributional metrics.
advanced.politicalStakes:
The most uncomfortable finding in the inequality literature — for democratic theory — is the evidence on political inequality. Martin Gilens's Affluence and Influence (2012) analyzed decades of survey data on Americans' policy preferences and found that government policy closely tracked the preferences of affluent Americans but was essentially unresponsive to the preferences of those with average or below-average incomes. When the affluent and the poor agreed, policy responded; when they disagreed, policy followed the affluent.

This finding is methodologically contested — critics argue that the analysis confounds preference intensity with preference content, and that the affluent's views may better track the policy consequences of proposed changes. But even critics who dispute the magnitude of the effect acknowledge that money has significant influence on political outcomes, and that this influence has increased as campaign finance restrictions have weakened.

The deeper problem is the feedback loop: high economic inequality creates high political inequality (because money amplifies political voice), which enables the wealthy to maintain policies that preserve or increase economic inequality. This feedback makes inequality self-reinforcing in ways that are difficult to interrupt through the ordinary political process — since the ordinary political process is itself shaped by inequality. Walter Scheidel's argument that inequality has historically been reduced primarily by catastrophe rather than reform is disturbing partly because it suggests this feedback loop is historically typical, not exceptional.
advanced.contestedHistoryC:
The most significant historiographical development in the study of inequality is the integration of very long-run data — going back centuries and in some cases millennia — with the detailed modern data assembled by Piketty, Saez, and their collaborators. Branko Milanovic's Global Inequality (2016) uses this data to document inequality trends at the global level: while inequality within rich countries has risen, inequality between countries has fallen as China, India, and other developing countries have grown rapidly. The net effect on global inequality is a shift in the distribution from a 'twin peaks' pattern (many poor people in poor countries, many rich people in rich countries, with a gap between) toward something more continuous — though still highly unequal.

The historical question of when inequality first emerged is being transformed by archaeological and genetic evidence. Studies of skeletal remains show clear markers of nutritional inequality in early agricultural societies; genetic studies of population structure can detect social stratification indirectly. The emerging picture is that substantial inequality — certainly of status and probably of material conditions — predates the written record. The claim that hunter-gatherer societies were egalitarian is being revised: they appear to have been less unequal than agricultural societies, but not equal.
advanced.whereDebateStands:
The contemporary policy frontier is the proposal for a global wealth tax, associated with Piketty and developed in technical detail by economists including Saez and Zucman. The practical obstacles are significant: wealth is easily hidden or moved across borders; international coordination is difficult; and defining and valuing wealth (particularly illiquid assets like private businesses) raises complex administrative questions. But the conceptual case — that income taxes are increasingly insufficient to address wealth concentration because wealth generates returns that income taxes do not capture — has been influential.

A separate frontier concerns predistribution: the idea that policy should shape market distributions before redistribution rather than only correcting them after the fact. Policies that strengthen workers' bargaining power (through labor law reform, sector-level bargaining, or profit-sharing requirements), shape corporate governance (through board representation for workers, for example), or provide universal capital endowments (like Thomas Piketty's proposed inheritance for all young adults) aim to prevent inequality from arising rather than compensating for it after it has arisen.

The normative frontier concerns what equality actually requires. The relational egalitarian literature suggests that the appropriate metric is not income or wealth but the social conditions of equal standing — that a society could become more equal in a meaningful sense without reducing income inequality if it ensured universal access to high-quality public goods, eliminated demeaning dependencies, and created conditions for genuine democratic participation. This reframing has significant implications for how we evaluate both current levels of inequality and proposed remedies.

foundation.theStory: Inequality is one of those concepts that seems simple — some people have more than others — but reveals enormous complexity on inspection. It has multiple dimensions (income, wealth, opportunity, status), multiple causes (technology, institutions, policy, history), and multiple normative frameworks (libertarian, egalitarian, relational) that evaluate it very differently. Understanding the contemporary inequality debate requires separating the empirical questions (how much inequality is there, what has driven its changes) from the normative questions (how much is too much, what should be done) — which are often conflated in political argument.
foundation.theDebate: The major scholarly debate concerns the explanation for the rise in inequality in rich countries since the 1970s. Skill-biased technological change, globalization, the decline of labor market institutions, and changes in corporate governance norms have all been proposed as primary causes, with different implications for policy. The second major debate concerns Piketty's long-run theory — whether r > g is a reliable enough tendency to generate predictions about the future trajectory of inequality, or whether institutional and political factors are too variable to support such a structural claim.
foundation.whyItMatters: Inequality connects to virtually every other major policy debate: taxation, education, healthcare, housing, labor markets, campaign finance, and democratic governance. Understanding the empirical and normative debates about inequality is prerequisite to serious engagement with any of these areas. It is also the context for understanding Social Darwinism (which naturalized inequality), the welfare state (which sought to reduce it), and contemporary debates about redistribution, meritocracy, and the relationship between economic and political power.
questions: Should we care about inequality of outcomes, inequality of opportunity, or both — and if both, what happens when policies that reduce one increase the other?

Is the rise in inequality since the 1970s primarily a product of technology and globalization (forces that are difficult to reverse) or of policy and institutional choices (forces that could be changed)?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The scholarly conversation on inequality runs across economics (Piketty, Saez, Zucman, Acemoglu, Autor), political science (Bartels, Gilens), philosophy (Rawls, Nozick, Cohen, Anderson, Scheffler), sociology (Tilly, Wright), and economic history (Scheidel, Milanovic). The major fault lines: (1) empirical — the relative weight of technology, globalization, and institutions in explaining rising inequality; (2) theoretical — whether r > g is a structural tendency or a contingent relationship; (3) normative — whether the appropriate framework is luck egalitarianism, prioritarianism, relational egalitarianism, or some other approach; (4) historical — whether the mid-20th-century compression of inequality was a durable institutional achievement or a transient disruption.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The evidence base for inequality research has improved dramatically with access to administrative tax records, which overcome survey underreporting of top incomes. What is well established: top income and wealth shares have risen significantly in most rich countries since the 1970s, most dramatically in the United States. What is contested: the causal mechanisms and their relative importance. What cannot be resolved with existing evidence: counterfactual questions about what inequality would have been under different policy regimes, and the direction of causality in the relationship between inequality and political outcomes.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about inequality must specify which dimension of inequality it is analyzing (income, wealth, opportunity, relational), which causal mechanism it is emphasizing, and which normative framework it is deploying. It must engage directly with the Piketty framework — either accepting r > g as the structural driver or explaining why institutional and political factors are more important. And it must take a position on the normative question: is inequality objectionable because of its sources (luck egalitarianism), its effects on the worst-off (prioritarianism), or what it does to social relationships (relational egalitarianism)?
questions: Does Piketty's r > g argument succeed as a structural theory of inequality, or does the historical evidence require a more institutionally specific account?

What would it mean for a society to take relational egalitarianism seriously as a political goal, and how would the policies it implies differ from those implied by luck egalitarianism or prioritarianism?

research.topic: The rise in top income and wealth concentration in the United States since 1970
research.content: Using tax records rather than surveys, economists Thomas Piketty, Emmanuel Saez, and Gabriel Zucman have documented a dramatic increase in income and wealth concentration in the United States since the 1970s. The top 1% income share roughly doubled from approximately 10% in the 1970s to approximately 20% by the 2010s. The top 0.1% wealth share returned to levels last seen in the 1920s. These findings have been replicated and extended by multiple researchers using independent data sources. The patterns are broadly confirmed for other rich countries, though the US case is the most extreme.

research.topic: The political consequences of economic inequality
research.content: A growing literature connects rising economic inequality to political polarization, democratic backsliding, and the rise of populist movements. The proposed mechanisms include: reduced social trust between economic groups, increased resentment among those left behind by economic changes, and the direct effects of economic insecurity on political behavior. Martin Gilens's work on policy responsiveness suggests that economic inequality has translated into political inequality. This literature is growing but the causal mechanisms are not fully established — the correlation between rising inequality and rising political polarization could reflect a common cause rather than a direct relationship.

research.topic: The precise magnitude of behavioral responses to redistributive taxation
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: large-scale randomized controlled trials in which tax rates are varied across otherwise identical populations, allowing direct measurement of behavioral responses. Why unavailable: tax policy cannot be randomized; natural experiments (countries or jurisdictions that have raised or lowered top tax rates) provide some evidence but involve too many confounding changes to isolate the behavioral response to taxation alone. What can be responsibly inferred: that there are behavioral responses to very high marginal tax rates, but that these responses are smaller than claimed by many critics of redistribution — the empirical literature consistently finds smaller elasticities than theoretical arguments would predict. What must remain unknowable: the precise revenue-maximizing top marginal tax rate and the magnitude of efficiency costs of redistribution at different levels.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Labor View
comparativeNarrative.content: For workers and the labor movement, inequality is not primarily a distributional puzzle but a power relationship. The employer and the employee do not meet as equals: the employer can wait longer without a job than the employee can wait without wages; the employer can hire another worker more easily than the worker can find another employer; the employer has lawyers and accountants, the worker typically does not. The growing gap between CEO and worker pay is not, from this perspective, a reflection of growing differences in contribution — it reflects the decline of unions, which once gave workers collective power to bargain on more equal terms. The appropriate response is not redistribution after the fact but restoring workers' power before income is distributed — through unions, collective bargaining, and labor law reform.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Libertarian View
comparativeNarrative.content: From a libertarian perspective, the focus on income and wealth inequality misdirects attention from more important questions of freedom and opportunity. Voluntary exchanges between consenting adults, whatever distribution they produce, are not in themselves unjust — the justice of an economic outcome cannot be read off the distribution alone but requires knowing how it came about. High inequality is not necessarily a problem: what matters is whether everyone has enough, whether exchanges are voluntary, and whether opportunities are genuinely open. The concern is that redistribution requires coercive taxation, imposes efficiency costs that reduce the total amount of wealth available, and can be captured by politically powerful groups at the expense of the vulnerable.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Global View
comparativeNarrative.content: The debate about inequality in rich countries focuses almost exclusively on within-country inequality, but the most extreme inequalities are between countries rather than within them. A person in the bottom 10% of the US income distribution is wealthier than the majority of people in the world. From a global perspective, the inequality debate in rich countries looks narrow: the relevant comparison is not between rich and poor Americans but between the billions of people living on a few dollars a day and the billions living in wealthy countries. This perspective suggests that migration — which allows workers from poor countries to access rich-country labor markets — is among the most powerful inequality-reducing policies available, though it is rarely framed in those terms in domestic political debate.

rabbitHole.label: Democracy
rabbitHole.reason: High economic inequality threatens political equality — one of the central tensions in democratic theory is whether meaningful democracy is possible when wealth is highly concentrated and money shapes political outcomes.

rabbitHole.label: Social Darwinism
rabbitHole.reason: Social Darwinism was the most influential ideology of inequality — arguing that unequal outcomes reflect differences in biological fitness and that redistribution interferes with natural selection.

rabbitHole.label: The Industrial Revolution
rabbitHole.reason: The Industrial Revolution produced the first modern form of extreme economic inequality — concentrating wealth in factory owners while creating a large propertyless working class — and generated the political movements that have structured the inequality debate ever since.

rabbitHole.label: The Communist Manifesto
rabbitHole.reason: The Manifesto was the founding text of the political movement most directly aimed at eliminating economic inequality — and its diagnosis of how capitalism produces class conflict remains one of the most influential frameworks for thinking about the political consequences of unequal distribution.

rabbitHole.label: Justice
rabbitHole.reason: The normative debate about inequality is inseparable from theories of justice — what Rawls, Nozick, and their successors argued about fairness directly determines whether any given level or form of inequality is just or unjust.

rabbitHole.label: Capital in the Twenty-First Century — Thomas Piketty (2014)
rabbitHole.reason: The most influential recent work on inequality — Piketty's data, his theory of r > g, and his proposal for a global wealth tax transformed the terms of the debate and are now the essential reference point for any serious engagement with the subject.

reference.author: Piketty, Thomas
reference.title: Capital in the Twenty-First Century
reference.reliability: High — data widely accepted; theoretical framework contested
reference.contribution: Transformed the empirical measurement of inequality and proposed the r > g theory of its long-run dynamics
reference.annotation: The essential reference point for any contemporary discussion of inequality. Piketty's data on historical income and wealth concentration is now the foundation of the field; his theoretical argument about r > g is more contested. Readers should engage with both the data and the critiques of the theory.

reference.author: Rawls, John
reference.title: A Theory of Justice
reference.contribution: The dominant liberal egalitarian framework for thinking about just distributions
reference.annotation: The most influential work of political philosophy of the 20th century and the essential starting point for normative thinking about inequality. Rawls's difference principle — that inequalities are just only if they benefit the least well-off — sets the terms for most subsequent liberal debate.

reference.author: Anderson, Elizabeth
reference.title: What Is the Point of Equality?
reference.contribution: The founding text of relational egalitarianism; challenges luck egalitarianism's focus on sources of inequality
reference.annotation: A landmark philosophical essay that redirected the inequality debate from questions about the sources of inequality to questions about its social consequences — specifically, whether inequality is compatible with the equal standing that democratic citizenship requires. Essential for understanding why equality matters beyond its effects on the worst-off.

reference.author: Scheidel, Walter
reference.title: The Great Leveler: Violence and the History of Inequality from the Stone Age to the Twenty-First Century
reference.reliability: High — disturbing historical argument, well evidenced
reference.contribution: Documents that throughout history, major reductions in inequality have been produced primarily by catastrophe rather than reform
reference.annotation: The most important long-run historical perspective on inequality. Scheidel's argument — that war, revolution, pandemic, and state collapse are the primary historical levelers — is deeply uncomfortable for those who believe that democratic reform can reduce inequality. The evidence is substantial; the implications are genuinely troubling.

commerce.title: Capital in the Twenty-First Century
commerce.author: Thomas Piketty
commerce.note: The essential reference — read at least the introduction and Parts III and IV even if the full 700 pages are ambitious.

commerce.title: The Great Leveler
commerce.author: Walter Scheidel
commerce.note: The best long-run historical perspective — sobering and essential for anyone who thinks democratic reform can straightforwardly address inequality.

commerce.title: The Tyranny of Merit
commerce.author: Michael Sandel
commerce.note: Connects the inequality debate to meritocracy — arguing that the assumption that market winners deserve their success is both empirically false and politically corrosive.

popularCulture.title: Inequality for All
popularCulture.description: Robert Reich's 2013 documentary traces the rise of income inequality in the United States and argues that it is both a product of political choices and a threat to democratic governance — the most accessible introduction to the empirical case.

popularCulture.title: Parasite
popularCulture.description: Bong Joon-ho's 2019 Palme d'Or winner uses the literal architecture of wealth and poverty — basement apartments, hilltop houses — to make visceral the social distances that economic inequality creates, and the violence latent in those distances.

popularCulture.title: The Grapes of Wrath
popularCulture.description: Steinbeck's 1939 novel remains the most powerful literary account of inequality in the Great Depression — capturing how economic catastrophe translates into human dignity stripped and human solidarity strained.
